articles.title: Ancient Legged Snake Rewrites Evolutionary History
articles.summary: A well-preserved fossil of the ancient snake Najash rionegrina, discovered in Argentina, provides new insights into snake evolution. This 100-million-year-old specimen had hind legs and a cheekbone (jugal bone), a feature lost in modern snakes. The study, published in Science Advances in 2019, used micro-CT scanning to reveal detailed skull anatomy, including nerve and blood vessel paths. The findings challenge the older hypothesis that snakes evolved from small burrowing ancestors. Instead, evidence suggests the ancestors of modern snakes were larger-bodied with wide mouths. The research also shows that early snakes retained their hindlimbs for an extended period before the evolution of limbless modern snakes. The study corrected a 160-year-old misunderstanding about the jugal bone in snakes. Later studies, including a 2020 discovery of a blind snake from Brazil and a 2023 brain reconstruction study, further support the idea that early snake evolution in Gondwana was diverse and often involved larger body sizes. A 2025 study on a Jurassic squamate from Scotland added more complexity, indicating that early squamate evolution involved anatomical experimentation and convergent evolution.

articles.title: 132-Million-Year-Old Dinosaur Tracks Discovered in South Africa
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered dinosaur tracks on a remote coastline in South Africa's Western Cape. The tracks are about 132 million years old, dating back to the early Cretaceous Period. This makes them the youngest known dinosaur tracks in southern Africa, 50 million years younger than any previously found in the Karoo Basin. The tracks were found in a small outcrop of the Brenton Formation, which is only about 40 meters long and 5 meters wide. Despite its small size, the site contains dozens of footprints, suggesting that dinosaurs were common in the area during the Cretaceous. The tracks include both flat impressions on rock surfaces and cross-sections within cliffs. The discovery is significant because the fossil record in southern Africa after massive volcanic eruptions around 182 million years ago is sparse. These tracks provide evidence that dinosaurs continued to thrive in the region long after the eruptions. The researchers, who are ichnologists specializing in fossil tracks, were initially looking for a dinosaur tooth but instead found the footprints. This is only the second set of Cretaceous dinosaur tracks ever found in South Africa and the second from the Western Cape.

articles.title: First Talks on Ending Fossil Fuel Use Amid UN Stalemate
articles.summary: A group of about 60 nations, including some major oil producers, are meeting in Santa Marta, Colombia, to discuss a complete transition away from fossil fuels. This is significant because UN climate conferences have failed to achieve such an agreement due to the need for unanimous consent, which gives large fossil fuel producers veto power. The meeting includes countries like Colombia, Australia, and Nigeria, but major powers such as the US, China, and India are absent. The urgency is driven by scientific warnings that global warming is accelerating; scientists predict the 1.5°C threshold could be breached within three to five years, leading to more severe climate impacts. Participants aim to show that a critical mass of countries supports renewable energy, hoping to encourage hesitant nations. The UK Climate Envoy emphasized sharing experience in transitioning to clean energy. The meeting is not meant to replace the COP process but to complement it, as frustration with slow progress grows.

articles.title: Hidden Voids Discovered in Menkaure Pyramid Suggest Secret Entrance
articles.summary: Researchers from Cairo University and the Technical University of Munich, working on the ScanPyramids project, have discovered two air-filled cavities behind the eastern facade of the Menkaure pyramid at Giza. The eastern side has long puzzled scientists because a section of granite blocks is unusually smooth and polished, similar to the known entrance on the north side. In 2019, researcher Stijn van den Hoven proposed that a second entrance might exist there. Using non-invasive techniques such as ground-penetrating radar, ultrasound, and electrical resistivity tomography, the team confirmed two voids at depths of 1.4 and 1.13 meters behind the outer wall. One void measures about 1 meter high and 1.5 meters wide, the other 0.9 by 0.7 meters. The data integration method called Image Fusion helped validate the findings. This discovery strengthens the hypothesis of a hidden entrance on the east side. Professor Christian Grosse from TUM noted that the methodology allows precise conclusions without damaging the structure. The ScanPyramids project previously found a hidden corridor in the Great Pyramid in 2023.

articles.title: Carbon Nanotube Wires Get a Boost: Could They Challenge Copper?
articles.summary: Carbon nanotubes are tiny cylindrical structures made of carbon atoms. They have remarkable properties, including high strength and the ability to conduct electricity. However, their electrical conductivity has been limited compared to metals like copper. Researchers in Spain have now developed a method to significantly improve the conductivity of carbon nanotube fibers. They used a chemical dopant called tetrachloroaluminate (AlCl4–) to add extra electrons to the nanotubes. The doping process increased the mean conductivity by a factor of 10, with the best fibers reaching about 70% of the conductivity of aluminum and nearly half that of copper. Importantly, because carbon nanotubes are very light, the doped fibers outperform copper when conductivity is normalized by density. However, the enhanced conductivity was not stable over time, as the material degraded. This research points toward potential applications in lightweight wiring for electronics, aerospace, and other fields where weight is critical.

articles.title: The Ongoing Quest to Measure Big G
articles.summary: The gravitational constant, known as Big G, is a fundamental constant that determines the strength of gravity. Despite centuries of effort, its value remains imprecise compared to other constants. Different experiments yield values that vary by about one part in 10,000. Gravity is the weakest of the four fundamental forces, making it difficult to measure in a lab due to background noise from Earth's gravity. In a recent study, scientists at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) replicated a 2007 experiment from the International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM) that had produced a divergent result. They built a torsion balance with metal cylinders and measured the gravitational torque. They also used an electrostatic method to twist the wire in the opposite direction for a second estimate. To ensure accuracy, they performed the experiment with both copper and sapphire masses, obtaining nearly identical values. However, their result still does not match other experiments, leaving the discrepancy unresolved. The team hopes that by carefully replicating one outlier, they can help identify sources of error and eventually achieve a more precise value for Big G.
